topics.title: Introduction to Various Stages of the Design Process: Formulation of Problem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET415 - COMPUTER AIDED DESIGN AND ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Various Stages of the Design Process: Formulation of Problem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is the "Design Process"?

The **Design Process** is a structured, iterative sequence of engineering and cognitive activities that transforms an identified human need or market opportunity into a fully defined, producible, and verifiable product, system, or component. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, the design process is not a single act of creativity but a **systematic engineering methodology** composed of well-defined stages — each stage acting as a gateway that must be satisfactorily cleared before proceeding to the next.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme — PEIET415 Module 1):**
> The *Design Process* is defined as a logical, sequential, and iterative engineering workflow that begins with the recognition of a societal/technical need and concludes with the production of a physical artifact or software system, encompassing the stages of **Need Identification, Problem Formulation, Conceptual Design, Preliminary Design, Detailed Design, Production, and Testing/Validation**.

## 1.2 The Big Picture: Stages of the Engineering Design Process

While minor variations exist across textbooks, the **standard KTU-recommended decomposition** of the design process (as per the 2024 Scheme syllabus of PEIET415) consists of the following sequential stages:

1. **Recognition of Need** — Identifying that a problem or opportunity exists.
2. **Problem Formulation / Definition** — Translating the need into a precise, quantified engineering problem.
3. **Concept Generation (Conceptual Design / Synthesis)** — Brainstorming alternative solution principles.
4. **Concept Evaluation & Selection** — Choosing the most promising concept.
5. **Preliminary (Configuration) Design** — Developing the chosen concept into a layout.
6. **Detailed Design / Embodiment Design** — Specifying all geometry, materials, and tolerances.
7. **Production / Manufacturing Planning** — Preparing for fabrication.
8. **Testing, Validation & Iteration** — Verifying the product against original needs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Emphasis:** The first two stages (Need Identification and **Problem Formulation**) collectively consume only **~10–15%** of total design time but define **~80%** of the final product's cost, performance, and feasibility. Hence, Module 1 places heavy weightage on these foundational stages.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "Designing a House"

Imagine you want to **build a new house** for your family. You would not simply hand a brick to a mason and say "start building." Instead, you would:

- **Recognize the Need:** "Our family is growing; we need a bigger home." (Recognition of Need)
- **Formulate the Problem:** "We need a 3-bedroom house, approximately 1800 sq.ft., within a budget of ₹50 lakhs, located in Kochi, with earthquake-resistant structure, by December 2026." (Problem Formulation)
- **Generate Concepts:** Sketch floor plans — *Ranch style, Duplex, Courtyard, Modern Villa* — and pick one.
- **Preliminary Design:** Approve the chosen floor plan, decide room positions, flow.
- **Detailed Design:** Decide the exact thickness of walls (230 mm), concrete grade (M25), electrical layout, plumbing, finishes.
- **Construction:** Actually build it.
- **Validation & Feedback:** Move in; if the kitchen ventilation is poor, **iterate** the design for the next project.

The same logic — **Need → Formulation → Concept → Detail → Build → Test** — applies to designing a turbine blade, an IC engine piston, an aircraft wing, or a finite element mesh.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid — The "RFP" Mnemonic:** The first three stages collapse into the acronym **RFP**, which mirrors industry terminology:
> - **R** — Recognition of Need
> - **F** — Formulation of Problem (the focus of this module topic)
> - **P** — Preliminary/Conceptual Design

## 1.4 What Exactly is "Problem Formulation"?

**Problem Formulation** is the second and arguably the most critical stage of the design process. It is the act of converting a vague, qualitative human need into a **precise, quantitative, and bounded engineering problem statement** that a designer (or a CAE/CAD software) can act upon.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition — Problem Formulation:**
> Problem formulation is the systematic process of identifying and documenting the design **objectives**, **constraints**, **design variables**, **performance criteria**, and **functional requirements** of an engineering artifact, so that the original need is translated into a mathematically and logically solvable problem.

It answers four fundamental questions:

1. **What** must the design achieve? *(Objectives)*
2. **What** is allowed to vary? *(Design Variables)*
3. **What** is forbidden or fixed? *(Constraints)*
4. **How** will success be measured? *(Performance Criteria)*

## 1.5 Importance of Problem Formulation in CAD

In the context of **Computer Aided Design and Analysis (PEIET415)**, problem formulation is the bridge between a real-world engineering problem and its **computational representation** — the geometry, the boundary conditions, the load cases, the material model, the optimization variables, and the convergence criteria that are fed into CAD/CAE software (SolidWorks, ANSYS, CATIA, Creo, Abaqus, MATLAB, etc.).

> [!WARNING]
> **Classic KTU Board Pitfall:** Many students begin CAD modeling or FEA simulation *without* explicitly formulating the problem. The result is a beautiful 3D model that solves the **wrong problem**. KTU examiners specifically look for evidence of a written problem formulation step in your answers.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Content

## 2.1 Detailed Stage-by-Stage Breakdown of the Design Process

Below is a high-yield, board-exam-ready analytical breakdown of every stage of the design process, with explicit focus (in **bold**) on the **Problem Formulation** stage.

### Stage 1 — Recognition of Need (Need Identification)

- **Purpose:** Recognize that a problem exists, a market opportunity exists, or a customer pain point exists.
- **Inputs:** Customer complaints, market surveys, technological breakthroughs, regulatory changes, competition.
- **Outputs:** A written *need statement* (e.g., "We need a lighter bicycle frame to win uphill races").
- **Who does it:** Marketing, end users, product managers, R&D leadership.
- **CAD/CAE Role:** Minimal at this stage; mostly ethnographic and qualitative research.

### Stage 2 — Problem Formulation / Definition ⭐ (Primary Focus of Module 1)

- **Purpose:** Convert the need statement into a precise engineering problem with quantified objectives and constraints.
- **Key Sub-Activities:**
  - **Information Gathering:** Literature review, patents, standards (BIS, ISO, ASTM), competitor benchmarking.
  - **Customer Requirements Translation:** Convert "light" → "mass ≤ 1.2 kg"; convert "durable" → "fatigue life ≥ 10⁷ cycles".
  - **Identification of Design Variables:** Parameters the designer is free to change.
  - **Identification of Constraints:** Physical laws, material limits, cost ceilings, regulatory limits.
  - **Definition of Objective Function(s):** A measurable quantity to be minimized or maximized.
  - **Establishment of Performance Criteria:** How the design will be judged.
  - **Functional Decomposition:** Breaking the overall function into sub-functions (FAST diagrams).
- **Outputs:** A **Design Brief / Problem Statement Document** containing objectives, constraints, variables, criteria, and assumptions.
- **Who does it:** Lead design engineer, systems engineer, in consultation with stakeholders.
- **CAD/CAE Role:** **Crucial.** This is where the *mathematical model* is born — variables, constraints, and objectives that will later be fed into FEA, CFD, or optimization software.

### Stage 3 — Conceptual Design (Synthesis)

- **Purpose:** Generate a broad set of alternative solution principles.
- **Techniques Used:** Brainstorming, Morphological charts, TRIZ, Biomimicry, Axiomatic Design (SUH), Synectics.
- **Outputs:** Multiple *concept sketches* and *principle solutions*.
- **CAD/CAE Role:** Conceptual CAD sketches, rough 3D block models.

### Stage 4 — Concept Evaluation and Selection

- **Purpose:** Filter concepts using Pugh matrices, decision matrices, weighted scoring, or feasibility analysis.
- **Outputs:** A single selected concept for further development.

### Stage 5 — Preliminary Design (Configuration / Embodiment)

- **Purpose:** Develop the chosen concept into a layout with major dimensions and configurations.
- **Outputs:** Layout drawings, preliminary FEA results, BOM (Bill of Materials) skeleton.

### Stage 6 — Detailed Design (Embodiment / Production Design)

- **Purpose:** Specify all geometry, tolerances, surface finish, materials, fasteners, GD&T.
- **Outputs:** Full engineering drawings, FEA/CFD validated models, manufacturing specifications.

### Stage 7 — Production / Manufacturing

- **Purpose:** Fabricate prototypes or production units.
- **Outputs:** Physical artifact.

### Stage 8 — Testing, Validation, and Iteration

- **Purpose:** Verify the artifact against original needs; iterate if failed.
- **Outputs:** Test reports, redesign triggers.

## 2.2 The Five Pillars of a Well-Formulated Problem

A properly formulated engineering problem (per KTU 2024 Module 1 expectations) must always contain the following five elements:

| # | Element | Symbol | Definition | Example (Pressure Vessel) |
|---|---------|--------|------------|---------------------------|
| 1 | **Design Variables** | $x = (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n)$ | Quantities the designer is free to choose | Wall thickness $t$, inner radius $r$, material grade |
| 2 | **Objective Function** | $f(x)$ | Scalar quantity to be minimized or maximized | Minimize mass $M(t, r)$ |
| 3 | **Constraints** | $g_i(x) \le 0$ | Inequality restrictions on the design | Hoop stress $\sigma_h \le \sigma_{allow}$ |
| 4 | **Bounds** | $x_L \le x \le x_U$ | Allowable ranges of variables | $5\text{ mm} \le t \le 20\text{ mm}$ |
| 5 | **Performance Criteria** | $P_j(x) \ge P_{j,\text{target}}$ | Metrics for success | Burst pressure $\ge 10$ MPa |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When asked to "formulate a problem" in a 7-mark or 14-mark question, students are expected to explicitly list **all five pillars** above. A common mistake is to mention only the objective and forget the constraints — this typically costs **2–3 marks**.

## 2.3 Mathematical Formulation Framework (Optimization View)

In CAE-driven design, problem formulation is expressed as a **constrained optimization problem** in the standard form:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Find } & \mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n) \\
\text{to minimize } & f(\mathbf{x}) \\
\text{subject to } & g_j(\mathbf{x}) \le 0, \quad j = 1, 2, \dots, m \\
& h_k(\mathbf{x}) = 0, \quad k = 1, 2, \dots, p \\
& x_i^L \le x_i \le x_i^U, \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, n
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $\mathbf{x}$ is the **vector of design variables**.
- $f(\mathbf{x})$ is the **objective function** (e.g., mass, cost, stress concentration factor).
- $g_j(\mathbf{x}) \le 0$ are **inequality constraints** (e.g., stress limits, deflection limits, geometric clearance).
- $h_k(\mathbf{x}) = 0$ are **equality constraints** (e.g., equilibrium equations, energy balance).
- $x_i^L$ and $x_i^U$ are the **lower and upper bounds** (side constraints) on each variable.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Mechanical Design (Pressure Vessels, Shafts, Beams):** Problem formulation defines the load cases, allowable stresses (per ASME Section VIII), and material choices.
- **Aerospace:** Mass minimization under stress, flutter, and thermal constraints.
- **Civil/Structural:** Cost minimization under deflection, seismic, and wind load constraints.
- **CAD Software Pipeline:** SolidWorks Simulation, ANSYS Workbench, and CATIA all require the user to explicitly define the problem — geometry, material, mesh, boundary conditions, loads, and output of interest — before any analysis can run.
- **Topology Optimization:** The objective (e.g., maximize stiffness), constraints (e.g., volume fraction ≤ 30%), and design space must be formulated first.

## 2.5 Common Pitfalls in Problem Formulation

1. **Vagueness:** Stating "make it strong" instead of "ultimate tensile strength ≥ 450 MPa".
2. **Conflicting Objectives:** Simultaneously demanding minimum cost *and* minimum mass *and* maximum life.
3. **Hidden Constraints:** Forgetting manufacturing constraints (e.g., minimum wall thickness for casting = 3 mm).
4. **Over-Constraining:** Defining so many constraints that the feasible region becomes empty (no solution exists).
5. **Wrong Objective Function:** Optimizing mass when the real objective is *cost per unit fatigue life*.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Framework: Building a Problem Formulation

> Since the topic is conceptual, this section provides an **exhaustive step-by-step framework** for formulating an engineering design problem, demonstrated using a canonical KTU-style example: **Design of a Cantilever Beam for Minimum Mass**.

## 3.1 Step 0 — The Original Need Statement

> *"We need a cantilever beam for a sensor mounting bracket in a portable weather station. The beam must support a 500 N load at its tip without excessive deflection, must fit within a 400 mm envelope, must cost less than ₹200, and must not fail in fatigue over a 5-year service life."*

## 3.2 Step 1 — Functional Decomposition

Identify the **primary function** and **secondary functions**:

- **Primary Function:** Carry a 500 N transverse tip load with controlled deflection.
- **Secondary Functions:** Resist fatigue, fit within envelope, remain affordable.

## 3.3 Step 2 — Identify Design Variables

Choose what the designer is free to vary:

$$
\mathbf{x} = (b, h, L, \rho, E, S_{ut})
$$

Where:
- $b$ = width of the rectangular cross-section (mm).
- $h$ = height of the rectangular cross-section (mm).
- $L$ = length of the cantilever (mm).
- $\rho$ = material density (kg/m³).
- $E$ = Young's modulus (GPa).
- $S_{ut}$ = ultimate tensile strength (MPa).

## 3.4 Step 3 — Formulate the Objective Function

For **minimum mass**, using volume × density:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f(\mathbf{x}) &= M = \rho \cdot V = \rho \cdot b \cdot h \cdot L
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.5 Step 4 — Quantify All Constraints

### 4a. Deflection Constraint

Maximum tip deflection of a cantilever under end load $P$ is:

$$
\delta_{\max} = \frac{P L^3}{3 E I}
$$

For a rectangular section, $I = \dfrac{b h^3}{12}$. Therefore:

$$
\delta_{\max} = \frac{P L^3}{3 E \cdot (b h^3 / 12)} = \frac{4 P L^3}{E b h^3}
$$

Allowable deflection (per sensor accuracy, say 2 mm):

$$
g_1(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{4 P L^3}{E b h^3} - \delta_{allow} \le 0
$$

Substituting $P = 500$ N, $\delta_{allow} = 2$ mm:

$$
g_1(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{4 \cdot 500 \cdot L^3}{E \cdot b \cdot h^3} - 2 \le 0
$$

### 4b. Static Strength Constraint

Maximum bending stress at the fixed end:

$$
\sigma_{\max} = \frac{M_{\text{bending}}}{Z} = \frac{P L}{(b h^2 / 6)} = \frac{6 P L}{b h^2}
$$

Allowable stress (with factor of safety $n = 2$):

$$
\sigma_{allow} = \frac{S_{ut}}{n}
$$

The constraint becomes:

$$
g_2(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{6 P L}{b h^2} - \frac{S_{ut}}{2} \le 0
$$

### 4c. Geometric (Envelope) Constraint

$$
g_3(\mathbf{x}) = L - 400 \le 0
$$

### 4d. Manufacturing Constraint (Minimum Thickness)

$$
g_4(\mathbf{x}) = 3 - b \le 0, \qquad g_5(\mathbf{x}) = 3 - h \le 0
$$

### 4e. Cost Constraint

Approximate cost per unit mass: $c$ ₹/kg. Mass $M = \rho b h L \times 10^{-9}$ (kg if dims in mm, density in kg/m³).

$$
g_6(\mathbf{x}) = c \cdot \rho \cdot b \cdot h \cdot L \cdot 10^{-9} - 200 \le 0
$$

## 3.6 Step 5 — Define Side Constraints (Bounds)

$$
\begin{aligned}
20 &\le b \le 60 \text{ mm} \\
20 &\le h \le 80 \text{ mm} \\
100 &\le L \le 400 \text{ mm} \\
2700 &\le \rho \le 7850 \text{ kg/m}^3 \quad \text{(Aluminum to Steel)} \\
70 &\le E \le 210 \text{ GPa} \\
250 &\le S_{ut} \le 600 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.7 Step 6 — Compact Mathematical Statement

The final, fully formulated problem is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Find } & \mathbf{x} = (b, h, L, \rho, E, S_{ut}) \\
\text{Minimize } & f(\mathbf{x}) = \rho \cdot b \cdot h \cdot L \\
\text{Subject to } & g_1: \dfrac{2000 L^3}{E \cdot b \cdot h^3} - 2 \le 0 \\
& g_2: \dfrac{3000 L}{b \cdot h^2} - \dfrac{S_{ut}}{2} \le 0 \\
& g_3: L - 400 \le 0 \\
& g_4: 3 - b \le 0 \\
& g_5: 3 - h \le 0 \\
& g_6: c \cdot \rho \cdot b \cdot h \cdot L \cdot 10^{-9} - 200 \le 0 \\
& 20 \le b \le 60, \;\; 20 \le h \le 80, \;\; 100 \le L \le 400 \\
& 2700 \le \rho \le 7850, \;\; 70 \le E \le 210, \;\; 250 \le S_{ut} \le 600
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.8 Step 7 — Final Engineering Problem Statement (Written Form)

> **Engineering Problem Statement:**
> *"Design a cantilever beam of minimum mass that supports a 500 N tip load on a span of at most 400 mm, with tip deflection not exceeding 2 mm and a factor of safety of at least 2 against static failure, manufactured at a cost not exceeding ₹200 per unit, using standard engineering materials available as rolled or extruded sections."*

This written statement, plus the mathematical formulation, constitutes a **complete, board-ready problem formulation** for a KTU 14-mark question.

## 3.9 Verification Checklist for Students

After formulating any problem, ask:

1. ✅ Have I identified **all** design variables?
2. ✅ Is my objective function **scalar, measurable, and unambiguous**?
3. ✅ Have I listed **every** relevant constraint (stress, deflection, buckling, thermal, manufacturing, cost)?
4. ✅ Are bounds **physically realistic**?
5. ✅ Does the problem have a **feasible region** (at least one solution exists)?
6. ✅ Can the problem be **solved by available CAE tools**?
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Flowchart — The Complete Design Process

The following Mermaid diagram depicts the full iterative design process, with the **Problem Formulation** stage explicitly highlighted as the focus of this module.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Stage 1: Recognition of Need"] --> B["Stage 2: Problem Formulation"]
    B --> C["Stage 3: Conceptual Design Synthesis"]
    C --> D["Stage 4: Concept Evaluation and Selection"]
    D --> E["Stage 5: Preliminary Configuration Design"]
    E --> F["Stage 6: Detailed Embodiment Design"]
    F --> G["Stage 7: Production and Prototyping"]
    G --> H["Stage 8: Testing Validation and Iteration"]
    H --> I{"Design Meets Need?"}
    I -- Yes --> J["Product Release"]
    I -- No --> B
    B -.Iterative Feedback.-> A
    F -.CAE FEA CFD.-> C
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The bold downward arrows represent the **forward sequential flow**, while the dotted arrows represent **iterative feedback loops** — a hallmark of real engineering design.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Internal Structure of Problem Formulation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PF1["Need Statement Qualitative"] --> PF2["Information Gathering"]
    PF2 --> PF3["Identify Design Variables"]
    PF2 --> PF4["Identify Constraints"]
    PF2 --> PF5["Define Objective Function"]
    PF2 --> PF6["Define Performance Criteria"]
    PF3 --> PF7["Mathematical Problem Model"]
    PF4 --> PF7
    PF5 --> PF7
    PF6 --> PF7
    PF7 --> PF8["Written Design Brief for CAD CAE"]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — The Five Pillars of a Formulated Problem

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1["Design Variables x vector"] --> PQ["Complete Formulated Problem"]
    P2["Objective Function f of x"] --> PQ
    P3["Constraints g of x le 0"] --> PQ
    P4["Side Constraints xL to xU"] --> PQ
    P5["Performance Criteria P targets"] --> PQ
```

## 4.4 Functional Architecture Flow — Inputs and Outputs of Problem Formulation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN1["Customer Requirements"] --> PROC["Problem Formulation Stage"]
    IN2["Engineering Standards ISO BIS"] --> PROC
    IN3["Material Databases"] --> PROC
    IN4["Regulatory Constraints"] --> PROC
    PROC --> OUT1["Quantified Design Variables"]
    PROC --> OUT2["Mathematical Objective Function"]
    PROC --> OUT3["Constraint Equations"]
    PROC --> OUT4["Design Brief Document"]
    OUT4 --> NEXT["Conceptual Design Stage"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]`
**List and briefly explain any three stages of the engineering design process.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Recognition of Need:** The first stage, where the existence of a problem, market opportunity, or customer pain point is identified. A qualitative need statement is produced (e.g., "we need a quieter exhaust fan").
2. **Problem Formulation:** The need is converted into a precise, quantified engineering problem with defined design variables, objective function, constraints, and performance criteria. A design brief is produced.
3. **Conceptual Design (Synthesis):** Multiple alternative solution principles are generated using techniques such as brainstorming, morphological charts, or TRIZ, and a promising concept is selected for further development.

*(Any three stages with brief explanation — full 3 marks.)*

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]`
**What is meant by "Problem Formulation" in the design process? Why is it considered the most critical stage?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Problem formulation is the systematic process of translating a qualitative need statement into a precise, quantified engineering problem by identifying the design variables, the objective function to be optimized, the constraints to be satisfied, and the performance criteria for success.

**Critical Importance (1.5 marks):** It is considered the most critical stage because errors or omissions made during formulation propagate through all subsequent stages of design, leading to products that are unsafe, uneconomical, non-functional, or unsolvable. Approximately 80% of a product's lifecycle cost and quality is determined by decisions made during the formulation stage, even though it consumes only 10–15% of the total design time.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU ESE Module 1 Choice A — 14 Marks]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain in detail the various stages of the engineering design process. Draw a neat flowchart showing the iterative nature of the process.

**Model Solution:**

The engineering design process consists of the following sequential stages (4 marks for description, 3 marks for the flowchart and iteration explanation):

1. **Recognition of Need:** Identify a customer problem or market gap. Output: qualitative need statement.
2. **Problem Formulation:** Quantify the need — design variables, objectives, constraints, performance criteria. Output: design brief.
3. **Conceptual Design:** Generate multiple solution principles using brainstorming, morphological analysis, TRIZ. Output: set of concept sketches.
4. **Concept Evaluation:** Use Pugh matrices or weighted scoring to select the best concept. Output: chosen concept.
5. **Preliminary Design:** Develop the chosen concept into a layout with major dimensions. Output: layout drawing, preliminary FEA.
6. **Detailed Design:** Specify all geometry, tolerances, materials, GD&T. Output: full engineering drawings, validated CAE models.
7. **Production:** Manufacture prototypes and production units. Output: physical artifact.
8. **Testing & Iteration:** Verify against original need; if it fails, **iterate back** to the relevant earlier stage.

**Flowchart (to be drawn on answer sheet):**
```
Need → Formulation → Concept → Evaluate → Preliminary → Detail → Produce → Test
                                                                    |
                                                                  (fail)
                                                                    v
                                                              [Iterate back]
```
The **iterative feedback loop** is essential — design is never strictly linear. *(3 marks for proper flowchart with iteration loop explicitly shown.)*

---

**(b) [7 marks]** For the design of a **two-leaf cantilever spring** to support a 1500 N load with maximum deflection of 25 mm, **formulate the complete engineering problem** (identify design variables, objective function, constraints, bounds, and performance criteria).

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Need Statement:** Design a lightweight, durable two-leaf cantilever spring to support a 1500 N central load with ≤ 25 mm deflection, for a small trailer suspension.

**Step 2 — Design Variables (1.5 marks):**
- $b$ = width of each leaf (mm)
- $t$ = thickness of each leaf (mm)
- $L$ = effective length of spring (mm)
- $n$ = number of leaves (= 2, given)
- $E$ = Young's modulus of material (GPa)
- $\sigma_{ut}$ = ultimate tensile strength (MPa)

**Step 3 — Objective Function (1.5 marks):** Minimize mass:
$$f(\mathbf{x}) = M = n \cdot \rho \cdot b \cdot t \cdot L$$

**Step 4 — Constraints (3 marks):**

- **Deflection constraint** (cantilever spring, central load $P$):
  $$\delta = \frac{P L^3}{4 E n b t^3} \le 25 \text{ mm}$$

- **Bending stress constraint** (with factor of safety $n_f = 1.5$):
  $$\sigma_{\max} = \frac{3 P L}{2 n b t^2} \le \frac{\sigma_{ut}}{1.5}$$

- **Manufacturing constraint:** $t \ge 5$ mm (minimum rolled thickness).

**Step 5 — Bounds (0.5 mark):**
$$25 \le b \le 75, \quad 5 \le t \le 20, \quad 300 \le L \le 800$$
$$E \in \{200 \text{ GPa (steel)}\}, \quad \sigma_{ut} \in \{400 \text{ to } 600 \text{ MPa}\}$$

**Step 6 — Performance Criteria (0.5 mark):**
- Static deflection ≤ 25 mm under 1500 N.
- Fatigue life ≥ 10⁶ cycles at working load.
- Mass < 4 kg.

This constitutes the **complete problem formulation** ready for CAE/optimization.

---

### Question B `[KTU ESE Module 1 Choice B — 14 Marks]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Discuss the **five essential elements** that must be present in any well-formulated engineering design problem, with one illustrative example for each.

**Model Solution:**

The five essential elements are (each element: 1.4 marks):

1. **Design Variables** — Quantities the designer is free to vary.
   *Example:* Wall thickness $t$, inner radius $r$, and material grade for a pressure vessel.

2. **Objective Function** — A scalar quantity to be minimized or maximized.
   *Example:* Minimize mass of the pressure vessel $M(t, r, \rho) = \rho \cdot 2\pi r L t$ (for a thin cylindrical shell).

3. **Constraints** — Inequality or equality conditions that must be satisfied.
   *Example:* Hoop stress $\sigma_h = \dfrac{Pr}{t} \le \sigma_{allow}$.

4. **Bounds (Side Constraints)** — Allowable ranges of the design variables.
   *Example:* $5 \text{ mm} \le t \le 25 \text{ mm}$; $100 \text{ mm} \le r \le 500 \text{ mm}$.

5. **Performance Criteria** — Metrics defining design success.
   *Example:* Burst pressure ≥ 1.5 × working pressure; corrosion life ≥ 20 years.

Together these five elements convert a vague need into a solvable optimization problem.

---

**(b) [7 marks]** A machine tool component is required to transmit 7.5 kW at 1440 rpm with a **factor of safety of 2.5**. Formulate the design problem for **minimum mass** of the shaft, clearly stating the design variables, objective function, constraints, and bounds. Assume standard C45 steel.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Need Statement:** Design a transmission shaft for minimum mass that can transmit 7.5 kW at 1440 rpm with adequate strength and stiffness.

**Step 2 — Design Variables (1.5 marks):**
- $d$ = shaft diameter (mm)
- $L$ = shaft length between bearings (mm)
- Material: C45 steel, $S_{ut} = 600$ MPa, $S_{sy} = 350$ MPa, $E = 210$ GPa, $\rho = 7850$ kg/m³.

**Step 3 — Torque and Loads (1 mark):**
$$T = \frac{60 P}{2 \pi N} = \frac{60 \cdot 7500}{2 \pi \cdot 1440} \approx 49.74 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 4 — Objective Function (1 mark):** Minimize mass:
$$f(d, L) = M = \rho \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot L$$

**Step 5 — Constraints (2.5 marks):**

- **Torsional strength constraint** (max shear stress with $n = 2.5$):
  $$\tau_{\max} = \frac{16 T}{\pi d^3} \le \frac{S_{sy}}{2.5}$$
  $$\frac{16 \cdot 49.74 \times 10^3}{\pi d^3} \le \frac{350}{2.5}$$
  $$\frac{253{,}300}{d^3} \le 140 \quad \Rightarrow \quad d^3 \ge 1809 \quad \Rightarrow \quad d \ge 12.2 \text{ mm}$$

- **Torsional rigidity (angle of twist) constraint** (≤ 0.5°/m):
  $$\frac{\theta}{L} = \frac{32 T}{\pi G d^4} \le \frac{0.5 \cdot \pi}{180 \cdot 1000}$$
  with $G = 80$ GPa.

- **Bearing span / slenderness constraint** to avoid whirling:
  $$\frac{L}{d} \le 50$$

**Step 6 — Bounds (1 mark):**
$$15 \le d \le 80 \text{ mm}, \quad 200 \le L \le 1500 \text{ mm}$$

**Final Formulation (verbal):** *"Find the shaft diameter $d$ and length $L$ that minimize mass while transmitting 7.5 kW at 1440 rpm with a factor of safety of 2.5 against torsional yielding, twist angle ≤ 0.5°/m, and slenderness ratio ≤ 50."*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks in "Design Process / Problem Formulation" Questions:**
>
> 1. **Drawing the flowchart but forgetting iteration (–2 marks).** KTU 2024 Scheme emphasizes *iterative* design — always include a feedback loop from testing back to formulation/concept.
> 2. **Listing variables without units or bounds (–1 mark).** Every design variable must carry its physical units and a numerical range.
> 3. **Writing a "vague" objective like "best design" (–1.5 marks).** KTU examiners demand a *scalar* objective function — e.g., "minimize mass in kg" or "minimize cost in ₹".
> 4. **Missing the factor of safety in strength constraints (–1 mark).** Always include the FoS in any stress-based constraint: $\sigma \le S_{yield}/n$.
> 5. **Not writing the final design brief as a single consolidated statement (–1 mark).** End every formulation question with a clear, written engineering problem statement.
> 6. **Confusing "need" with "problem" (–1 mark).** Need = qualitative (e.g., "lightweight bicycle"). Problem = quantitative (e.g., "frame mass ≤ 1.2 kg, fatigue life ≥ 10⁷ cycles").

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- 🔑 The design process is an **iterative**, not strictly linear, sequence of stages.
- 🔑 The **eight canonical stages** are: Need Recognition → Problem Formulation → Conceptual Design → Concept Evaluation → Preliminary Design → Detailed Design → Production → Testing & Iteration.
- 🔑 **Problem Formulation** translates a qualitative need into a quantitative, solvable engineering problem.
- 🔑 A properly formulated problem must contain **five elements**: Design Variables, Objective Function, Constraints, Bounds, and Performance Criteria.
- 🔑 The mathematical standard form is: **Minimize** $f(\mathbf{x})$ **subject to** $g_j(\mathbf{x}) \le 0$, $h_k(\mathbf{x}) = 0$, and $x_i^L \le x_i \le x_i^U$.
- 🔑 **~80%** of a product's lifecycle cost and quality is decided during the formulation stage, even though it consumes only **10–15%** of total design time.
- 🔑 In CAE, problem formulation is the **bridge** between the real-world engineering need and the computational model (geometry, material, mesh, boundary conditions, loads, solver settings).
- 🔑 Common pitfalls: vague objectives, missing constraints, conflicting objectives, empty feasible regions.
- 🔑 Always end your answer with a **written, consolidated engineering problem statement** — this is what KTU examiners award the last 1–2 marks for.
- 🔑 The acronym **RFP** (Recognition – Formulation – Preliminary) is a useful memory aid for the first three stages.
- 🔑 The iterative nature of the design process must always be **explicitly shown** in any flowchart drawn in the exam.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Generation of Alternatives
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET415 - COMPUTER AIDED DESIGN AND ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Generation of Alternatives

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Generation of Alternatives** is the systematic phase of the engineering design process in which the designer, supported by **Computer-Aided Design (CAD)** tools, decomposes a design problem into functional sub-problems and synthesizes a *plurality of feasible, conceptually distinct solution concepts* for each sub-function before merging them into complete, integrated design proposals.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme (PEIET415 – Module 1), this phase sits between **Problem Definition** and **Concept Evaluation**. It is the *creative-divergent* stage of design, where the *solution space* is explored broadly rather than converged upon a single answer.

Key syllabus-aligned vocabulary:

| Term | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| **Functional Decomposition** | Breaking the overall design function into elementary sub-functions (e.g., *transform, transmit, store, support, guide*). |
| **Conceptual Variant** | A structurally or behaviourally different way of fulfilling one sub-function. |
| **Design Solution Space** | The $n$-dimensional hyper-rectangle spanned by all independent design variables. |
| **Feasible Region** | The subset of the design space satisfying all constraints. |
| **Pareto-Optimal Alternative** | A non-dominated design; no other feasible alternative is superior in every objective. |

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain-English Picture)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Many Roads to the Same City" Metaphor**
> Imagine you must travel from **Calicut** to **Trivandrum**. You can choose a car, a train, a bus, a flight, or even a bicycle. Each mode is an **alternative**; the *generation of alternatives* phase is when you sit down and list **all** these possible modes *before* you compare their ticket price, duration, and comfort. CAD does the same thing — except instead of travel modes, it explores **gears, shafts, materials, or circuit topologies** that can all satisfy the same engineering requirement.

A second analogy useful in design theory: it is the **"menu-creation stage"** in a restaurant — the chef brainstorms *many recipes* (alternatives), then only later *evaluates* which one makes it onto the menu. Skipping this stage leads to *premature fixation* on a single solution, one of the most common reasons for engineering-design failure.

### 1.3 Why This Stage Matters in CAD

Modern CAD platforms (SolidWorks, CATIA, Fusion 360, ANSYS DesignModeler, Siemens NX) embed the alternative-generation philosophy directly through:

- **Parametric/feature trees** — every dimension is a *decision variable*; modifying it spawns a new alternative automatically.
- **Configuration tables** — multiple design variants coexist in a single file.
- **Topology-optimisation solvers** — algorithmically generate thousands of geometric alternatives within a loadable envelope.
- **Generative design tools** — AI-driven exploration of the design space.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Under the KTU 2024 NEP-aligned Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework, this topic is mapped to **CO1 — *Apply the fundamentals of computer-aided design to synthesise engineering solutions*.** Questions testing the *systematic methodology* (rather than memorised lists of methods) carry the highest marks.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five-Stage Logical Flow of Alternative Generation

The phase is **not** random brainstorming. It follows a *rational, sequential* logic that maps directly onto CAD workbenches:

1. **Establish Overall Function (F):** What must the artefact *do*? (e.g., "transmit 5 kW at 1450 rpm with a step-down ratio of 3:1").
2. **Functional Decomposition:** Break $F$ into elementary sub-functions $f_1, f_2, \ldots, f_k$ using a *Function Analysis System Technique (FAST) diagram*.
3. **Means-Search (Concept Generation):** For each $f_i$, enumerate $n_i$ candidate *means* (physical working principles). Methods used:
   - **Brainstorming / Brainwriting** (Osborn, 1953)
   - **Synectics** (Gordon, 1961) — forcing analogies from nature (biomimicry), fantasy, or other engineering fields.
   - **TRIZ** (Altshuller, 1946) — contradiction matrix & 40 inventive principles.
   - **Morphological Analysis** (Zwicky, 1948).
   - **Systematic Search via Design Catalogs** (Roth, Pahl & Beitz).
4. **Combine Means into Complete Concepts (Synthesis):** Select one candidate means per sub-function and combine them into a *complete design concept*.
5. **Document & Screen:** Eliminate clearly infeasible concepts (e.g., violates a hard constraint such as $\text{cost} \leq \text{budget}$).

### 2.2 Morphological Chart — The Backbone of Systematic Alternative Generation

A **Morphological Chart** (or *Zwicky Box*) is a tabular tool where:

- **Rows** = sub-functions $f_i$.
- **Columns** = candidate means for each sub-function.
- **Cells** = specific solution variants.

A *path* through the chart (one cell per row) yields one **complete alternative**. The combinatorial richness of this method comes from the fact that even a modest chart can yield thousands of distinct alternatives.

### 2.3 Mathematical Formulation of the Solution Space

Let the design problem be decomposed into $k$ sub-functions. Suppose sub-function $f_i$ admits $n_i$ candidate means. Then the **total number of combinatorially possible complete alternatives** is:

$$
N_{\text{comb}} \;=\; \prod_{i=1}^{k} n_i
$$

However, not all of these are *feasible*. Let the *feasibility predicate* $\Phi(\mathbf{x}) \in \{0,1\}$ indicate whether design vector $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)$ satisfies the constraints. The number of feasible alternatives is:

$$
N_{\text{feas}} \;=\; \sum_{j=1}^{N_{\text{comb}}} \Phi\!\left(\mathbf{x}_j\right)
$$

In a CAD environment, the design variables $x_j$ are typically continuous (e.g., shaft diameter in mm). The *continuous* design space is:

$$
\mathcal{D} \;=\; \left\{\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n \;\middle|\; g_j(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0,\; j = 1, \ldots, m \right\}
$$

where $g_j(\mathbf{x})$ are the $m$ inequality constraints and equality constraints are handled similarly with $h_k(\mathbf{x}) = 0$.

### 2.4 Pareto Optimality (Multi-Objective Generation)

When alternatives are evaluated on $p$ conflicting objectives $f_1(\mathbf{x}), \ldots, f_p(\mathbf{x})$ (e.g., *minimise mass*, *minimise cost*, *maximise stiffness*), a single "best" rarely exists. Instead we obtain a **Pareto front**:

> [!NOTE]
> **Pareto-Dominance Definition:** A design $\mathbf{x}^{(a)}$ *dominates* $\mathbf{x}^{(b)}$, written $\mathbf{x}^{(a)} \prec \mathbf{x}^{(b)}$, if it is at least as good in every objective and strictly better in at least one:
> $$\forall\, i \in \{1,\ldots,p\}:\; f_i\!\left(\mathbf{x}^{(a)}\right) \leq f_i\!\left(\mathbf{x}^{(b)}\right) \;\;\text{and}\;\; \exists\, i:\; f_i\!\left(\mathbf{x}^{(a)}\right) < f_i\!\left(\mathbf{x}^{(b)}\right)$$
> A design is **Pareto-optimal** if no feasible design dominates it. The set of all Pareto-optimal designs is the **Pareto front** $\mathcal{P}^{\ast}$.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Symbol / Expression | Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|
| $k$ | Number of decomposed sub-functions | dimensionless |
| $n_i$ | Number of candidate means for sub-function $f_i$ | dimensionless |
| $N_{\text{comb}} = \prod_{i=1}^{k} n_i$ | Total combinatorial alternatives (morphological chart) | dimensionless |
| $N_{\text{feas}}$ | Number of constraint-satisfying alternatives | dimensionless |
| $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ | Design variable vector (continuous space) | mixed SI |
| $\mathcal{D} = \{\mathbf{x} \mid g_j(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0\}$ | Feasible region defined by $m$ constraints | mixed SI |
| $f_i(\mathbf{x})$ | $i$-th design objective | mixed SI |
| $\mathbf{x}^{(a)} \prec \mathbf{x}^{(b)}$ | Pareto-dominance relation | — |
| $\mathcal{P}^{\ast}$ | Pareto-optimal set / Pareto front | — |
| $\vert \mathcal{P}^{\ast} \vert$ | Cardinality of the Pareto front (number of non-dominated designs) | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anti-Pipe Rule:** Throughout the KTU valuation scripts, table cells that require a *vertical bar* (absolute value, set cardinality, divisibility) **must** be written as $\vert \cdot \vert$ or $\mid \cdot \mid$ — the raw $\vert$ symbol collapses the markdown table and breaks valuation automation.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Generation of alternatives is *the* phase that separates **engineering design** from **engineering analysis**. Its real-world payoff:

- **Automotive crashworthiness:** Generative design tools explore thousands of bumper-beam topologies; only Pareto-optimal ones survive.
- **Aerospace bracket design:** Airbus A320 family brackets, redesigned via generative CAD, achieved 30–45 % mass reduction while remaining feasible.
- **VLSI floorplanning:** CAD tools synthesise millions of placement alternatives; the chosen one minimises wire-length *and* thermal hotspots.
- **Civil/structural:** Topology-optimised bridge girders and building connection plates are direct industrial outputs of algorithmic alternative generation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Combinatorial Count of Alternatives from a Morphological Chart

**Statement.** Given $k$ sub-functions, each admitting $n_i$ candidate means, show that the total number of complete alternatives obtainable by choosing one means per sub-function is $N_{\text{comb}} = \prod_{i=1}^{k} n_i$.

**Derivation.**

Consider a sequence of choices $(c_1, c_2, \ldots, c_k)$ where $c_i$ is the index of the chosen means for sub-function $f_i$, and $c_i \in \{1, 2, \ldots, n_i\}$.

Step 1 — For sub-function $f_1$, there are exactly $n_1$ choices.

Step 2 — For each such choice of $f_1$, sub-function $f_2$ admits $n_2$ independent choices. By the **multiplicative principle of counting**, the partial count for the first two sub-functions is:

$$
N_2 \;=\; n_1 \cdot n_2
$$

Step 3 — Induction. Suppose after choosing means for the first $(i-1)$ sub-functions we have $N_{i-1}$ partial alternatives. Adding sub-function $f_i$ multiplies the count by $n_i$:

$$
N_i \;=\; N_{i-1} \cdot n_i \;=\; \left(\prod_{j=1}^{i-1} n_j\right) \cdot n_i
$$

Step 4 — Continuing up to $i = k$:

$$
N_k \;=\; \prod_{i=1}^{k} n_i \;\equiv\; N_{\text{comb}} \qquad \blacksquare
$$

**Numerical sanity check.** Take $k = 3$ sub-functions with $n_1 = 4, n_2 = 3, n_3 = 5$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{comb}} &= 4 \times 3 \times 5 \\
&= 12 \times 5 \\
&= 60 \text{ alternatives}
\end{aligned}
$$

If even one more sub-function ($n_4 = 2$) is added:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{comb}} &= 60 \times 2 = 120 \text{ alternatives}
\end{aligned}
$$

This **exponential-like growth** explains why systematic enumeration is impossible by hand and motivates the use of CAD/CAx tools for the exploration step.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Feasibility Filter (Reducing $N_{\text{comb}}$ to $N_{\text{feas}}$)

**Statement.** Show that $N_{\text{feas}} \leq N_{\text{comb}}$, with equality only when every combinatorial alternative is feasible.

**Derivation.**

Define the feasibility indicator for the $j$-th complete alternative $\mathbf{x}_j$:

$$
\Phi(\mathbf{x}_j) \;=\; \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } g_r(\mathbf{x}_j) \leq 0 \;\; \forall\, r \in \{1,\ldots,m\} \\[4pt] 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

Step 1 — Sum over all combinatorial alternatives:

$$
N_{\text{feas}} \;=\; \sum_{j=1}^{N_{\text{comb}}} \Phi(\mathbf{x}_j)
$$

Step 2 — Because each $\Phi(\mathbf{x}_j) \in \{0,1\}$, every term contributes either 0 or 1. Hence:

$$
0 \;\leq\; N_{\text{feas}} \;\leq\; \sum_{j=1}^{N_{\text{comb}}} 1 \;=\; N_{\text{comb}}
$$

Step 3 — The upper bound is attained iff $\Phi(\mathbf{x}_j) = 1$ for all $j$, i.e., the constraint set is non-binding over the entire combinatorial space — typically only true in unconstrained academic problems. In real engineering, $N_{\text{feas}} \ll N_{\text{comb}}$.

**Engineering implication.** Even after a feasibility filter, the surviving alternatives may be in the *hundreds or thousands*. This is why **screening matrices, weighted-scoring methods, and Pareto-based multi-objective optimisation** are essential follow-on steps.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Pareto Front in a 2-Objective Problem (Bi-objective)

**Statement.** For two objectives $f_1(\mathbf{x})$ (minimise mass) and $f_2(\mathbf{x})$ (minimise cost), show that the Pareto front in the objective space is a *monotonically non-increasing* locus.

**Derivation.**

Step 1 — Assume two Pareto-optimal designs $\mathbf{x}^{(a)}$ and $\mathbf{x}^{(b)}$ with $f_1(\mathbf{x}^{(a)}) < f_1(\mathbf{x}^{(b)})$. By Pareto-optimality of $\mathbf{x}^{(b)}$, there must be no design dominating it; in particular $\mathbf{x}^{(a)}$ cannot dominate $\mathbf{x}^{(b)}$.

Step 2 — For $\mathbf{x}^{(a)}$ to dominate $\mathbf{x}^{(b)}$ we would require $f_2(\mathbf{x}^{(a)}) \leq f_2(\mathbf{x}^{(b)})$. Since domination does *not* hold, we must have:

$$
f_2\!\left(\mathbf{x}^{(a)}\right) \;>\; f_2\!\left(\mathbf{x}^{(b)}\right)
$$

Step 3 — Therefore, as $f_1$ increases along the Pareto front, $f_2$ must decrease. Equivalently, the front is **monotonically decreasing** in the bi-objective objective space:

$$
\frac{df_2}{df_1} \;\leq\; 0 \quad \text{along } \mathcal{P}^{\ast}
$$

Step 4 — The same argument generalises to $p$ objectives: any increase along one objective axis on the Pareto front must be compensated by non-degradation (and typically strict improvement) along at least one other axis — the hallmark of the **trade-off surface**.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Expected Diversity of a Random Search Baseline

**Statement.** If we generate $M$ alternatives uniformly at random in a hypercube $\mathcal{H} \subset \mathbb{R}^n$, the expected number that fall inside a feasible region $\mathcal{D}$ of hyper-volume $V_\mathcal{D}$ inside $\mathcal{H}$ of volume $V_\mathcal{H}$ is:

$$
\mathbb{E}[N_{\text{feas hit}}] \;=\; M \cdot \frac{V_\mathcal{D}}{V_\mathcal{H}}
$$

**Derivation.**

Step 1 — The probability that a single uniformly drawn sample lies inside $\mathcal{D}$ is, by the geometric definition of probability:

$$
p \;=\; \frac{V_\mathcal{D}}{V_\mathcal{H}}
$$

Step 2 — The number of "hits" in $M$ independent trials follows a binomial distribution $N_{\text{hit}} \sim \mathrm{Binomial}(M, p)$.

Step 3 — The expectation of a binomial random variable is $M \cdot p$:

$$
\mathbb{E}[N_{\text{hit}}] \;=\; M \cdot p \;=\; M \cdot \frac{V_\mathcal{D}}{V_\mathcal{H}} \qquad \blacksquare
$$

**Engineering interpretation.** This result is the *theoretical baseline* against which smarter algorithms (genetic algorithms, simulated annealing, gradient-based topology optimisation) are judged. Smart alternative-generation strategies must achieve a **higher expected hit rate** than the random baseline to justify their computational cost.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Method Selection Guide

| Method | Type of Logic | When to Use | Typical Output Count | CAD Tool Support |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Brainstorming | Intuitive / Divergent | Early concept stage, small team | 10 – 50 | Whiteboard / Mind-map |
| Synectics | Intuitive / Analogical | Need breakthrough innovation | 5 – 20 | Manual |
| TRIZ | Systematic / Patent-based | Engineering contradictions | 10 – 100 | Software (CREC, Invention Tool) |
| Morphological Chart | Systematic / Combinatorial | Multi-function problems | $\prod_i n_i$ (often 100s) | Spreadsheet / Custom CAD |
| Design Catalog (Pahl-Beitz) | Systematic / Reuse | Standard machine elements | 20 – 200 | KISSsoft, MDESIGN |
| Generative Design (AI) | Algorithmic | Continuous design space | $10^3$ – $10^6$ | Fusion 360, NX, CATIA |
| Topology Optimisation | Algorithmic | Lightweight structures | $10^2$ – $10^4$ mesh variants | ANSYS, Altair Inspire |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Block Diagram — The Alternative-Generation Pipeline

The following Mermaid block diagram traces the *functional architecture* of how a CAD system ingests a design brief, expands it into alternatives, and feeds them into the downstream evaluation phase.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Design Brief and Specifications] --> B1[Establish Overall Function F]
    B1 --> C1[Functional Decomposition into Sub Functions]
    C1 --> D1[Means Search per Sub Function]
    D1 --> D2[Intuitive Methods Brainstorming Synectics]
    D1 --> D3[Systematic Methods TRIZ Morphological]
    D1 --> D4[Algorithmic Methods Topology Optim Generative]
    D2 --> E1[Build Morphological Chart]
    D3 --> E1
    D4 --> E1
    E1 --> F1[Synthesize Complete Concepts]
    F1 --> G1[Apply Feasibility Filter]
    G1 --> H1[Document Alternatives as CAD Models]
    H1 --> I1[Hand Over to Concept Evaluation Phase]
    style A1 fill:#E8F0FE,stroke:#1A56DB
    style I1 fill:#E6F4EA,stroke:#137333
```

### 4.2 Morphological Chart Architecture (Block-Level View)

The chart below shows how a *3-sub-function* problem (e.g., a simple gear reducer) is decomposed. Each row represents one sub-function; each cell holds a candidate means; a *path* selects one means per row to build a complete alternative.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    RowA[Sub Function F1 Transmit Torque] --> CellA1[Gears]
    RowA --> CellA2[Belt Drive]
    RowA --> CellA3[Chain Drive]
    RowB[Sub Function F2 Support Shaft] --> CellB1[Rolling Bearing]
    RowB --> CellB2[Sliding Bearing]
    RowC[Sub Function F3 Connect to Motor] --> CellC1[Coupling Rigid]
    RowC --> CellC2[Coupling Flexible]
    CellA1 --> PathX[Alternative Path 1 Gear plus Bearing plus Rigid Coupling]
    CellA2 --> PathY[Alternative Path 2 Belt plus Sliding plus Flexible]
    CellB1 --> PathX
    CellB2 --> PathY
    CellC1 --> PathX
    CellC2 --> PathY
    style PathX fill:#FFF4E5,stroke:#B45309
    style PathY fill:#FFF4E5,stroke:#B45309
```

**Reading the chart.** With $n_1 = 3$, $n_2 = 2$, $n_3 = 2$, the total combinatorial count is:

$$
N_{\text{comb}} \;=\; 3 \times 2 \times 2 \;=\; 12 \text{ distinct reducer concepts}
$$

A CAD engineer may now prototype each as a 3-D model, run FEA, and select the Pareto-front members.

### 4.3 Decision Logic for Selecting a Generation Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Is the problem well defined and standard?} -->|Yes| Q2{Do standard catalogs exist?}
    Q1 -->|No| Q3{Need breakthrough innovation?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| R1[Use Design Catalog Pahl Beitz]
    Q2 -->|No| R2[Use Morphological Chart]
    Q3 -->|Yes| R3[Apply Synectics or TRIZ]
    Q3 -->|No| R4[Use Brainstorming plus Brainwriting]
    R1 --> O[Generate Alternatives]
    R2 --> O
    R3 --> O
    R4 --> O
    style O fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32
```

### 4.4 Pareto-Front Visualisation (Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Obj1[Objective 1 Minimise Mass] --> PF[Pareto Front Trade Off Locus]
    Obj2[Objective 2 Minimise Cost] --> PF
    Dom[Design A] --> PF
    Sub[Design B] --> PF
    Inf[Design C Infeasible] --> X[Excluded by Constraints]
    PF --> Sel[Designer Selects Based on Preferences]
    style PF fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#0D47A1
    style X fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#B71C1C
    style Sel fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#1B5E20
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

**Q1. Define "Generation of Alternatives" in the context of computer-aided engineering design. List any four systematic methods used for generating alternatives. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
*Generation of Alternatives* is the design phase in which multiple conceptually distinct solutions are systematically synthesised for a stated engineering problem, using CAD-supported decomposition of the overall function into sub-functions and enumeration of candidate *means* for each.

Four systematic methods:

1. Morphological Analysis (Zwicky Box).
2. TRIZ (Theory of Inventive Problem Solving).
3. Synectics.
4. Systematic Search via Design Catalogues (Pahl & Beitz).

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** Examiners award 1 mark for the definition and ½ mark per method (rounded). Avoid vague terms like *"thinking of ideas"* — use the exact syllabus phrasing.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**

**Q2. What is a Morphological Chart? How is the total number of combinatorially possible alternatives computed from it? (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
A *Morphological Chart* is a tabular representation in which the rows correspond to the elementary sub-functions obtained by decomposing the overall design function, and the columns enumerate candidate *means* (working principles) for each sub-function. A complete design concept is formed by selecting one cell from each row.

If the $i$-th sub-function admits $n_i$ candidate means, then the total number of distinct alternatives is:

$$
N_{\text{comb}} \;=\; \prod_{i=1}^{k} n_i
$$

where $k$ is the number of sub-functions. *(2 marks for formula and 1 mark for explanation.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – End Semester, Module 1 — Question Paper Pattern]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the step-by-step methodology for the *generation of alternatives* in engineering design. Use a suitable engineering example (e.g., design of a 5 kW gear reducer or a residential water purifier) to illustrate **functional decomposition**, **means search**, and **morphological synthesis**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Step 1 — *Define the overall function F.* For a 5 kW, 1450 rpm-to-480 rpm gear reducer: $F$ = "transmit 5 kW of mechanical power with a step-down speed ratio of approximately 3:1, enclosed in a housing, with input/output shafts aligned and lubricated."

Step 2 — *Functional decomposition.* Use a FAST diagram:

$$
F \;=\; \{\text{Transmit Torque},\; \text{Change Speed Ratio},\; \text{Support Shafts},\; \text{Contain Lubricant},\; \text{Couple to Motor and Load}\}
$$

This gives $k = 5$ sub-functions.

Step 3 — *Means search per sub-function* (illustrative table):

| Sub-function $f_i$ | Candidate Means ($n_i$ choices) |
|---|---|
| Transmit torque | Gears / Belt / Chain / Magnetic coupling (4) |
| Change speed ratio | Spur pair / Helical pair / Worm pair / Planetary (4) |
| Support shafts | Rolling bearing / Sliding bearing / Magnetic bearing (3) |
| Contain lubricant | Splash / Forced splash / Oil pump (3) |
| Couple to motor/load | Rigid coupling / Flexible coupling / Fluid coupling (3) |

Step 4 — *Morphological synthesis.* Total combinatorial alternatives:

$$
N_{\text{comb}} \;=\; 4 \times 4 \times 3 \times 3 \times 3 \;=\; 432 \text{ distinct concepts}
$$

Step 5 — *Feasibility screen.* Apply hard constraints (cost $\leq$ ₹ 25,000; mass $\leq$ 30 kg; input must be electric motor; efficiency $\geq$ 90 %). This typically reduces $N_{\text{feas}}$ to 20 – 40.

Step 6 — *Document surviving alternatives as CAD models.* Use parametric templates; each dimension is a design variable $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n$.

*(Allocate ~2 marks for steps 1–2, ~3 marks for the means table + chart construction, ~2 marks for combinatorial calculation + feasibility filter.)*

---

**(b)** With the help of a labelled sketch or block diagram, describe the **Pareto-optimal set** concept in multi-objective design. Show mathematically how two objectives trade off along the Pareto front and explain why a designer must make a *preference-based* final selection. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Step 1 — Define Pareto-dominance:
$$
\mathbf{x}^{(a)} \prec \mathbf{x}^{(b)} \iff \big[\forall i,\; f_i(\mathbf{x}^{(a)}) \leq f_i(\mathbf{x}^{(b)})\big] \;\land\; \big[\exists i,\; f_i(\mathbf{x}^{(a)}) < f_i(\mathbf{x}^{(b)})\big]
$$

Step 2 — For $p = 2$ objectives (mass $m$, cost $C$), the Pareto front is a *monotonically non-increasing* curve in the $(m, C)$ plane. Sketch the front with:

- $m$ on the horizontal axis (lower = better).
- $C$ on the vertical axis (lower = better).
- A typical non-dominated front that bends from upper-left to lower-right.

Step 3 — Show that as mass is reduced (moving leftward), cost rises (moving upward), reflecting the trade-off:

$$
\frac{dC}{dm} \;\leq\; 0 \quad \text{along } \mathcal{P}^{\ast}
$$

Step 4 — Explain that *all* Pareto-optimal designs are mathematically equivalent — there is no "best" without designer preference. Use the **compromise programming** or **weighted-sum** approach:

$$
\min_{\mathbf{x}} \; \sum_{i=1}^{p} w_i \, f_i(\mathbf{x}), \qquad w_i \geq 0,\; \sum_i w_i = 1
$$

where weights $w_i$ encode designer/stakeholder preference.

Step 5 — Conclude: the *generation* phase produces the **set** of alternatives; the *evaluation* phase (covered in a later module) narrows it via scoring; the *decision* phase (with human input) commits to one. *(Allocate ~2 marks for definition/sketch, ~3 marks for derivation/trade-off, ~2 marks for preference/decision discussion.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** A design problem has been decomposed into **four** sub-functions with **5, 3, 4, 2** candidate means respectively. Compute the total number of combinatorially possible alternatives. If a feasibility filter eliminates 88 % of these, how many feasible alternatives remain? Explain why this *combinatorial explosion* justifies the use of CAD-based generative design tools. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Step 1 — Apply the multiplicative counting formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{comb}} &= n_1 \cdot n_2 \cdot n_3 \cdot n_4 \\
&= 5 \times 3 \times 4 \times 2 \\
&= 15 \times 4 \times 2 \\
&= 60 \times 2 \\
&= 120 \text{ alternatives}
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 2 — Apply the 88 % elimination (i.e., 12 % survive):

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{feas}} &= 0.12 \times N_{\text{comb}} \\
&= 0.12 \times 120 \\
&= 14.4
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $N_{\text{feas}}$ must be an integer, the practical interpretation is **14 or 15 feasible alternatives** depending on rounding.

Step 3 — Discussion of combinatorial explosion:

Even this modest problem yields $N_{\text{comb}} = 120$ alternatives; realistic machine-design problems with $k \geq 8$ and $n_i \geq 4$ regularly yield:

$$
N_{\text{comb}} \;\geq\; 4^8 \;=\; 65{,}536 \text{ alternatives}
$$

Manual evaluation of each is infeasible. CAD tools — parametric feature trees, configuration tables, generative-design solvers, and topology-optimisation engines — exploit *parallel evaluation, GPU acceleration, and surrogate modelling* to explore such spaces in hours rather than years. This justifies their centrality in modern engineering practice and explains their inclusion in the KTU 2024 PEIET415 syllabus.

*(Allocate ~2 marks for formula + computation, ~1 mark for feasible count, ~4 marks for discussion.)*

---

**(b)** Explain the role of **TRIZ** and **Synectics** as systematic methods for generating engineering alternatives. Compare and contrast the two approaches in terms of their origin, underlying logic, and typical output. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**TRIZ (Theory of Inventive Problem Solving) — Genrich Altshuller, 1946.**

- **Origin:** Statistical analysis of thousands of worldwide patents to extract recurring patterns of invention.
- **Logic:** Most engineering problems contain *contradictions* (improve one parameter → worsen another). TRIZ codifies these into a **39 × 39 Contradiction Matrix** mapping each pair to one or more of the **40 Inventive Principles** (e.g., segmentation, taking out, local quality, the other way round).
- **Typical output:** *Technically novel* solutions that bypass trade-offs (e.g., segmented magnetic couplings instead of mechanical gears to eliminate lubrication needs).

**Synectics — William J.J. Gordon, 1961.**

- **Origin:** Psychological study of creative-thinking processes in industrial R&D teams (e.g., Arthur D. Little, Inc.).
- **Logic:** Force the designer to view the problem through *forced analogies* — direct, personal, symbolic, or fantasy analogies — to overcome mental fixedness.
- **Typical output:** *Conceptually radical* solutions often inspired by nature (biomimicry). Example: a self-cleaning surface modelled on lotus leaves; a high-strength lightweight structure modelled on bird bones.

**Comparison Matrix:**

| Aspect | TRIZ | Synectics |
|---|---|---|
| Origin | Patent statistics (USSR, 1946) | Group psychology studies (USA, 1961) |
| Underlying logic | Algorithmic — contradiction matrix | Psychological — analogy forcing |
| Output | Technically optimised, often evolutionary | Conceptually novel, often revolutionary |
| Best suited to | Well-defined engineering contradictions | Open-ended, ill-structured problems |
| CAD support | Strong (CREC, Goldfire Innovis) | Weak (manual brainstorming) |

**Conclusion:** TRIZ is *systematic-algorithmic* and is best used when a clear engineering contradiction has been identified; Synectics is *intuitive-analogical* and is best used in the very early, divergent phase of concept generation. Both are complementary — many modern industrial design teams use Synectics to seed ideas and TRIZ to refine them. *(Allocate ~3 marks for TRIZ, ~3 marks for Synectics, ~1 mark for the comparison and conclusion.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Confusing "generation" with "evaluation."** The generation phase produces alternatives — it does *not* score, rank, or select. Mixing the two phases costs up to **3 marks** in a 14-mark question.
> 2. **Forgetting the formula derivation.** Examiners specifically test $N_{\text{comb}} = \prod_i n_i$. A *correct formula with the wrong numerical evaluation* still receives partial credit; a *correct number without the formula* often does not.
> 3. **Treating TRIZ and Synectics as synonyms.** They are distinct in origin, logic, and output. Conflating them is an automatic deduction of **2 marks**.
> 4. **Skipping the Pareto-dominance definition.** When a question mentions "non-dominated" or "Pareto-optimal," the formal inequality $\mathbf{x}^{(a)} \prec \mathbf{x}^{(b)}$ must appear — a verbal-only explanation loses marks.
> 5. **Markdown formatting in Mermaid diagrams** (only relevant if the question requires a sketch). Always use plain uppercase text inside Mermaid node labels — bold or italics inside quotes breaks the renderer and earns a **0** for that sub-part.
> 6. **Using raw vertical bars** $\vert x \vert$ inside answer-table cells — write $\vert x \vert$ or $\mid x \mid$ to keep table syntax intact in digital valuation scripts.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Definition.** *Generation of Alternatives* = systematic synthesis of multiple distinct concepts for a decomposed engineering problem.
- **Position in design process.** Sits between *Problem Definition* and *Concept Evaluation* in the Pahl–Beitz / VDI 2225 model.
- **Three families of methods.** Intuitive (Brainstorming, Synectics), Systematic (TRIZ, Morphological), Algorithmic (Generative design, Topology optimisation).
- **Morphological Chart.** Rows = sub-functions, columns = candidate means. Total alternatives = $\prod_i n_i$.
- **Feasibility filter.** $N_{\text{feas}} = \sum_j \Phi(\mathbf{x}_j) \;\leq\; N_{\text{comb}}$.
- **Pareto-dominance.** $\mathbf{x}^{(a)} \prec \mathbf{x}^{(b)}$ iff better-or-equal in all objectives and strictly better in at least one.
- **Pareto front property.** In bi-objective problems, $\frac{df_2}{df_1} \leq 0$ along $\mathcal{P}^{\ast}$.
- **Random-search baseline.** $\mathbb{E}[N_{\text{hit}}] = M \cdot V_\mathcal{D} / V_\mathcal{H}$.
- **CAD tool integration.** Parametric trees, configuration tables, generative design, topology optimisation — all are modern *computational instantiations* of the alternative-generation philosophy.
- **Engineering payoff.** Reduces premature fixation, exposes hidden trade-offs, enables mass-efficient and cost-efficient Pareto-optimal solutions (Airbus A320 bracket: ~30 – 45 % weight reduction).
- **Common KTU errors.** Don't confuse generation with evaluation; don't omit the combinatorial formula; don't conflate TRIZ with Synectics.
- **Always remember the cardinality notation.** $\vert \mathcal{P}^{\ast} \vert$ — count of non-dominated designs.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Evaluation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET415 - COMPUTER AIDED DESIGN AND ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Evaluation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition
In the context of Computer Aided Design and Analysis (PEIET415), **Evaluation** refers to the systematic, multi-criteria assessment of design alternatives, concepts, or prototypes against a defined set of engineering, economic, manufacturability, and sustainability criteria to identify the most viable solution that best satisfies the design intent and customer requirements. It is the *judgment phase* of the design process where CAD models, simulations, and decision matrices are used to converge from a large solution space to an optimal or near-optimal final design.

Mathematically, evaluation is represented as a multi-attribute decision-making (MADM) problem where each candidate design $D_i$ is scored against criteria $C_j$ using weights $w_j$:

$$ E(D_i) = \sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j \cdot S_{ij} $$

where $E(D_i)$ is the overall evaluation score of design $i$, $w_j$ is the weight of criterion $j$ (with $\sum w_j = 1$), and $S_{ij}$ is the normalized score of design $i$ against criterion $j$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** Evaluation is a high-yield topic under the "Design Process and Evaluation Methodology" unit. Questions typically appear as 3-mark definitions and 14-mark descriptive problems on evaluation methods like the Pugh Matrix, Weighted Scoring, and Decision Trees.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine you are buying a smartphone. You have 5 models in front of you. You list what matters: **camera quality, battery life, price, processor speed, and brand trust**. You then decide how important each feature is to you (e.g., camera = 30%, battery = 25%, price = 20%, processor = 15%, brand = 10%). You score each phone on every feature out of 10 and multiply by the weight. The phone with the highest weighted total wins.

**Engineering design evaluation works exactly the same way.** Instead of phones, we evaluate CAD models. Instead of personal preferences, we use customer requirements (QFD — Quality Function Deployment), engineering standards, and manufacturability constraints. The CAD system helps us *generate* the alternatives, but the *evaluation* phase is what selects the winner.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Metrics in Evaluation:**
> - **DFA (Design for Assembly)** index
> - **DFM (Design for Manufacturability)** rating
> - **Cpk (Process Capability Index)** — typical threshold $\geq$ **1.33**
> - **Weight Criterion Score** in **percentage (%)** typically summing to **100%**
> - **Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ)** in monetary units

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Role of Evaluation in the Engineering Design Lifecycle
Evaluation is not a one-time event; it is iterative and embedded across the entire design lifecycle. It transforms subjective design intuition into objective, quantitative decision-making.

**Stages at which evaluation occurs:**
- **Conceptual Stage** — Evaluation of design concepts using decision matrices.
- **Embodiment Stage** — Evaluation of configurations, layouts, and material choices.
- **Detailed Stage** — Evaluation of FEA results, tolerances, and manufacturing simulations.

### 2.2 Types of Evaluation Methods
The following structured methods are commonly used and form the core of KTU questions:

1. **Screening (Go / No-Go)** — Quick binary filter to eliminate infeasible designs.
2. **Scoring (Weighted Sum Model)** — Quantitative rating against weighted criteria.
3. **Ranking (Ordinal)** — Designs are placed in order of preference.
4. **Decision Matrix (Pugh Matrix)** — Uses a *baseline* or *datum* design and compares others as $(+)$, $(-)$, or $(S)$ (same).
5. **Analytic Hierarchy Process (AHP)** — Pairwise comparison of criteria using **Saaty's 1–9 scale**.
6. **Cost-Benefit Analysis** — Evaluates designs on a pure monetary basis.
7. **Trade-off Analysis** — Identifies how improving one criterion affects another.

### 2.3 Step-by-Step Logic of the Weighted Scoring Method
- **Step 1: Define Criteria ($C_j$)**: Identify $n$ evaluation criteria from customer requirements (QFD House of Quality).
- **Step 2: Assign Weights ($w_j$)**: Each criterion is assigned an importance weight such that $\sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j = 1$.
- **Step 3: Generate Alternatives ($D_i$)**: Use CAD systems to create $m$ design alternatives.
- **Step 4: Score Each Alternative ($S_{ij}$)**: Rate each design on each criterion (typically 1 to 10, then normalize).
- **Step 5: Compute Weighted Score**: Apply $E(D_i) = \sum w_j \cdot S_{ij}$.
- **Step 6: Select Optimal Design**: The alternative with the maximum $E(D_i)$ is selected.
- **Step 7: Sensitivity Analysis**: Check whether the ranking is robust to small changes in $w_j$.

### 2.4 Pugh Matrix Logic
- Choose a *datum* (baseline) design.
- For each alternative $D_i$ and criterion $C_j$, mark:
  - **+** (better than datum)
  - **−** (worse than datum)
  - **S** (same as datum)
- Sum up the **+**, **−**, and **S** for each design.
- Designs with the most **+** marks and fewest **−** marks advance.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Description | Units / Scale |
|---|-------------------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | $E(D_i) = \sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j \cdot S_{ij}$ | Weighted evaluation score of design $i$ | Dimensionless (0 to 1 or 0 to 100) |
| 2 | $\sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j = 1$ | Normalization constraint on weights | Unitless |
| 3 | $S_{ij}^{norm} = \dfrac{S_{ij} - S_{j}^{min}}{S_{j}^{max} - S_{j}^{min}}$ | Min-max normalization of raw scores | Dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| 4 | $AHP_{ij} = \dfrac{w_i}{w_j}$ | Pairwise comparison ratio (Saaty 1–9) | Ratio scale |
| 5 | $CR = \dfrac{CI}{RI} < 0.10$ | Consistency Ratio must be $<$ 0.10 | Dimensionless |
| 6 | $CI = \dfrac{\lambda_{max} - n}{n-1}$ | Consistency Index | Dimensionless |
| 7 | $ROI = \dfrac{Net\ Benefit}{Total\ Cost} \times 100\%$ | Return on Investment for cost-based evaluation | Percentage (%) |
| 8 | $Cpk = \min\left(\dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}, \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$ | Process Capability Index | Dimensionless |
| 9 | $N_{parts} = \sum_{k} n_k$ | Total part count (lower is better in DFA) | Count |
| 10 | $DFA_{index} = \dfrac{N_{min}}{N_{actual}} \times 100\%$ | Design for Assembly efficiency | Percentage (%) |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Utility:** Weighted evaluation matrices are used by automotive OEMs (e.g., Toyota, Tata Motors) to select chassis designs, by aerospace firms (Boeing, Airbus) to select wing configurations, and by electronics companies to choose PCB layouts. CAD systems integrate these matrices with simulation results (FEA, CFD) for fully digital evaluation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Normalization of Raw Scores
Raw scores from different criteria are on different scales (e.g., cost in ₹, weight in kg, stress in MPa). To combine them fairly, we normalize them.

**Given:** Raw score $S_{ij}$ for design $i$ on criterion $j$.

**Step 1:** Identify the minimum and maximum raw scores across all $m$ designs for criterion $j$:
$$ S_j^{min} = \min_i(S_{ij}) \qquad S_j^{max} = \max_i(S_{ij}) $$

**Step 2:** For "higher-is-better" criteria (e.g., strength):
$$ S_{ij}^{norm} = \frac{S_{ij} - S_j^{min}}{S_j^{max} - S_j^{min}} $$

**Step 3:** For "lower-is-better" criteria (e.g., cost, weight), reverse the sign:
$$ S_{ij}^{norm} = \frac{S_j^{max} - S_{ij}}{S_j^{max} - S_j^{min}} $$

**Step 4:** Substitute into the weighted sum model:
$$ E(D_i) = \sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j \cdot S_{ij}^{norm} $$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)
**Problem:** A CAD team has generated **3 design alternatives** $D_1$, $D_2$, $D_3$ for a gearbox housing. The **3 evaluation criteria** and their weights are:

| Criterion | Weight $w_j$ | Type |
|-----------|--------------|------|
| $C_1$: Cost (lower better) | **0.40** | Minimize |
| $C_2$: Stiffness (higher better) | **0.35** | Maximize |
| $C_3$: Manufacturability (higher better) | **0.25** | Maximize |

Raw scores (out of 10):

| Design | $C_1$ (Cost) | $C_2$ (Stiffness) | $C_3$ (Manufacturability) |
|--------|--------------|-------------------|----------------------------|
| $D_1$ | 7 | 6 | 8 |
| $D_2$ | 5 | 9 | 6 |
| $D_3$ | 8 | 5 | 9 |

**Step 1 — Identify Min/Max for each criterion:**

For $C_1$ (Cost, lower-better):
$$ S_1^{min} = 5 \quad (\text{Design } D_2), \qquad S_1^{max} = 8 \quad (\text{Design } D_3) $$

For $C_2$ (Stiffness, higher-better):
$$ S_2^{min} = 5 \quad (D_3), \qquad S_2^{max} = 9 \quad (D_2) $$

For $C_3$ (Manufacturability, higher-better):
$$ S_3^{min} = 6 \quad (D_2), \qquad S_3^{max} = 9 \quad (D_3) $$

**Step 2 — Normalize using min-max formulas:**

*Lower-better criterion $C_1$ (Cost):*
$$ S_{11}^{norm} = \frac{8 - 7}{8 - 5} = \frac{1}{3} \approx 0.333 $$
$$ S_{21}^{norm} = \frac{8 - 5}{8 - 5} = \frac{3}{3} = 1.000 $$
$$ S_{31}^{norm} = \frac{8 - 8}{8 - 5} = \frac{0}{3} = 0.000 $$

*Higher-better criterion $C_2$ (Stiffness):*
$$ S_{12}^{norm} = \frac{6 - 5}{9 - 5} = \frac{1}{4} = 0.250 $$
$$ S_{22}^{norm} = \frac{9 - 5}{9 - 5} = \frac{4}{4} = 1.000 $$
$$ S_{32}^{norm} = \frac{5 - 5}{9 - 5} = \frac{0}{4} = 0.000 $$

*Higher-better criterion $C_3$ (Manufacturability):*
$$ S_{13}^{norm} = \frac{8 - 6}{9 - 6} = \frac{2}{3} \approx 0.667 $$
$$ S_{23}^{norm} = \frac{6 - 6}{9 - 6} = \frac{0}{3} = 0.000 $$
$$ S_{33}^{norm} = \frac{9 - 6}{9 - 6} = \frac{3}{3} = 1.000 $$

**Step 3 — Compute weighted scores $E(D_i) = \sum w_j \cdot S_{ij}^{norm}$:**

For $D_1$:
$$ E(D_1) = (0.40 \times 0.333) + (0.35 \times 0.250) + (0.25 \times 0.667) $$
$$ E(D_1) = 0.1333 + 0.0875 + 0.1667 = 0.3875 $$

For $D_2$:
$$ E(D_2) = (0.40 \times 1.000) + (0.35 \times 1.000) + (0.25 \times 0.000) $$
$$ E(D_2) = 0.4000 + 0.3500 + 0.0000 = 0.7500 $$

For $D_3$:
$$ E(D_3) = (0.40 \times 0.000) + (0.35 \times 0.000) + (0.25 \times 1.000) $$
$$ E(D_3) = 0.0000 + 0.0000 + 0.2500 = 0.2500 $$

**Step 4 — Decision:**

$$ E(D_2) = 0.7500 > E(D_1) = 0.3875 > E(D_3) = 0.2500 $$

**Therefore, Design $D_2$ is selected** as the optimal gearbox housing design.

**Step 5 — Sensitivity check:** If the weight of $C_3$ were increased to 0.40 and $C_1$ reduced to 0.25, recompute and confirm the ranking still holds. This is the *robustness test* expected in KTU 14-mark answers.

### 3.3 Analytical Summary Table

| Design | $E(D_i)$ | Rank | Verdict |
|--------|----------|------|---------|
| $D_1$ | 0.3875 | 2 | Reserve |
| $D_2$ | **0.7500** | **1** | **Selected** |
| $D_3$ | 0.2500 | 3 | Rejected |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Evaluation Workflow Block Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Define Design Problem] --> B[Identify Customer Requirements via QFD]
    B --> C[Generate Design Alternatives using CAD]
    C --> D[Define Evaluation Criteria C1, C2, ... Cn]
    D --> E[Assign Weights wj to Criteria]
    E --> F[Score Each Design Sij 1 to 10]
    F --> G[Normalize Scores using Min-Max]
    G --> H[Compute Weighted Sum E Di]
    H --> I{Sensitivity Check CR < 0.10}
    I -- Yes --> J[Select Best Design]
    I -- No --> K[Revise Weights and Recompute]
    K --> E
    J --> L[Proceed to Detailed CAD / FEA Validation]
    L --> M[End: Final Design]
```

### 4.2 Pugh Matrix Conceptual Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Baseline Datum Design] --> B[Compare D1 vs Datum]
    A --> C[Compare D2 vs Datum]
    A --> D[Compare D3 vs Datum]
    B --> E{Tally Plus, Minus, Same}
    C --> E
    D --> E
    E --> F[Design with Most Plus Marks Wins]
    F --> G[Move to Weighted Scoring]
    G --> H[Final Selection]
```

### 4.3 Evaluation Criteria Hierarchy (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph TD
    ROOT[Design Evaluation] --> T1[Technical Performance]
    ROOT --> T2[Economic Factors]
    ROOT --> T3[Manufacturability]
    ROOT --> T4[Sustainability]
    T1 --> S1[Strength]
    T1 --> S2[Stiffness]
    T1 --> S3[Weight]
    T2 --> S4[Initial Cost]
    T2 --> S5[Life Cycle Cost]
    T2 --> S6[ROI]
    T3 --> S7[DFM Index]
    T3 --> S8[DFA Index]
    T3 --> S9[Process Capability Cpk]
    T4 --> S10[Recyclability]
    T4 --> S11[Energy Efficiency]
    T4 --> S12[Emissions]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] Define the term "Evaluation" in the context of Computer Aided Design. List any two evaluation methods.**

**Model Answer:**
Evaluation in CAD is the systematic process of assessing design alternatives against multiple criteria such as cost, performance, manufacturability, and sustainability to identify the most suitable design. The two common methods are:
1. **Weighted Scoring Method** — assigns weights to criteria and rates each design numerically.
2. **Pugh Matrix** — uses a baseline (datum) design and compares alternatives as better, worse, or same.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024] What is the role of normalization in design evaluation?**

**Model Answer:**
Design criteria are measured on different units (e.g., cost in ₹, weight in kg, stress in MPa). Normalization converts all raw scores to a common dimensionless scale (typically 0 to 1) using the min-max formula:

$$ S_{ij}^{norm} = \frac{S_{ij} - S_j^{min}}{S_j^{max} - S_j^{min}} \quad \text{(for higher-better)} $$

This ensures fair comparison and prevents criteria with large numerical ranges from dominating the weighted sum.

---

### 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the **Weighted Scoring Method** for design evaluation. List its advantages and limitations.

**(b)** [7 Marks] For a CAD design problem, three alternatives $D_1$, $D_2$, $D_3$ are evaluated against four criteria with the following data:

| Criterion | Weight | $D_1$ | $D_2$ | $D_3$ | Type |
|-----------|--------|-------|-------|-------|------|
| $C_1$: Strength | 0.30 | 8 | 6 | 9 | Higher better |
| $C_2$: Cost | 0.25 | 5 | 8 | 6 | Lower better |
| $C_3$: Weight | 0.20 | 7 | 5 | 4 | Lower better |
